skills.skill: A: use the composition of two functions to model and solve real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers use the composition of two functions to model and solve real-world problems for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(A)). Function composition f(g(x)) substitutes g(x) into f. Composition is generally not commutative: f(g(x)) is usually different from g(f(x)). Key vocabulary includes: composition, domain, f(g(x)), inner function, outer function. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: A: Composition of functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers composition of functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(A)). Function composition f(g(x)) substitutes g(x) into f. Composition is generally not commutative: f(g(x)) is usually different from g(f(x)). Key vocabulary includes: composition, domain, f(g(x)), inner function, outer function. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: B: demonstrate that function composition is not always commutative
skills.prompt: This topic covers demonstrate that function composition is not always commutative for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(B)). Function composition f(g(x)) substitutes g(x) into f. Composition is generally not commutative: f(g(x)) is usually different from g(f(x)). Key vocabulary includes: composition, domain, f(g(x)), inner function, outer function. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: C: represent a given function as a composite function of two or more functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers representing a function as a composite of two or more functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(C)). Students decompose a complex function into simpler components: e.g., h(x)=sqrt(3x+1) can be written as f(g(x)) where f(x)=sqrt(x) and g(x)=3x+1. Key vocabulary: composition, decompose, inner function, outer function. Multiple valid decompositions may exist. Common errors: choosing decompositions that do not compose correctly, and domain issues.

skills.skill: D: describe symmetry of graphs of even and odd functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers describe symmetry of graphs of even and odd functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(D)). Even functions satisfy f(-x) = f(x) and are symmetric about the y-axis. Odd functions satisfy f(-x) = -f(x) and are symmetric about the origin. Key vocabulary includes: even function, odd function, origin, symmetry, y-axis. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: D: Even and odd functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers even and odd functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(D)). Even functions satisfy f(-x) = f(x) and are symmetric about the y-axis. Odd functions satisfy f(-x) = -f(x) and are symmetric about the origin. Key vocabulary includes: even function, odd function, origin, symmetry, y-axis. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: D: Symmetry and periodicity of trigonometric functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers symmetry and periodicity of trigonometric functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(D)). Sine is odd: sin(-x)=-sin(x). Cosine is even: cos(-x)=cos(x). Sin and cos have period 2pi; tan has period pi. Students use these properties to simplify expressions and evaluate functions. Key vocabulary: symmetry, periodicity, even, odd, period, 2pi, pi. Common errors: confusing even/odd properties, and not recognizing periodicity.

skills.skill: E: determine an inverse function, when it exists, for a given function over its domain or a subset of its domain and represent the inverse using multiple representations
skills.prompt: This topic covers determine an inverse function, when it exists, for a given function over its domain or a subset of its domain and represent the inverse using multiple representations for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(E)). Inverse functions undo each other: f(f^(-1)(x)) = x. Only one-to-one functions have inverses. Graphically, inverses reflect over y = x. Key vocabulary includes: f^(-1)(x), horizontal line test, inverse function, one-to-one, reflection, y = x. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: E: Identify inverse functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers identify inverse functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(E)). Inverse functions undo each other: f(f^(-1)(x)) = x. Only one-to-one functions have inverses. Graphically, inverses reflect over y = x. Key vocabulary includes: f^(-1)(x), horizontal line test, inverse function, one-to-one, reflection, y = x. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

Only one-to-one functions have inverses. Graphically, inverses reflect over y = x. Key vocabulary includes: f^(-1)(x), horizontal line test, inverse function, one-to-one, reflection, y = x. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: E: Check whether two rational functions are inverses
skills.prompt: This topic covers check whether two rational functions are inverses for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(E)). Inverse functions undo each other: f(f^(-1)(x)) = x. Only one-to-one functions have inverses. Graphically, inverses reflect over y = x. Key vocabulary includes: f^(-1)(x), horizontal line test, inverse function, one-to-one, reflection, y = x. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: F: graph exponential, logarithmic, rational, polynomial, power, trigonometric, inverse trigonometric, and piecewise defined functions, including step functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers graphing exponential functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(F)). For f(x) = ab^x: plot y-intercept (0, a), draw horizontal asymptote y = 0, create table of values, sketch curve. For b > 1 (growth): rises left to right. For 0 < b < 1 (decay): falls left to right. Key points: (0, a), (1, ab), (-1, a/b). The function never crosses the asymptote (for a > 0). With vertical shift d, asymptote moves to y = d. Vocabulary: exponential function, asymptote, growth, decay, y-intercept, end behavior. Common errors: drawing curve crossing the asymptote, not plotting enough points, and forgetting to shift asymptote.

skills.skill: F: Graph piecewise-defined functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers evaluating piecewise-defined functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(F)). A piecewise function has different rules for different domain intervals. To evaluate: determine which piece applies based on the input value, then use that formula. Check boundary points carefully for which piece includes them. Example: f(x) = {x+1 if x<0; 2x if x>=0}. Vocabulary: piecewise function, domain restriction, boundary, interval. Common errors: using wrong piece, misreading inequalities at boundaries, and not evaluating boundary points correctly.

skills.skill: F: Graph a quadratic function
skills.prompt: This topic covers graphing quadratic functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(F)). Students graph f(x)=ax^2+bx+c or vertex form, identifying vertex, axis of symmetry, direction, intercepts, and domain/range. In Precalculus, emphasis on analyzing key attributes and connecting to transformations of parent function y=x^2. Key vocabulary: parabola, vertex, axis of symmetry, direction, intercepts. Common errors: vertex calculation mistakes, and not identifying all key features.

skills.skill: F: Match polynomials and graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers match polynomials and graphs for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(F)). Match/graph polynomials by analyzing: degree (determines end behavior and max turning points), leading coefficient (determines direction), zeros/x-intercepts, y-intercept, and multiplicity of roots. Key vocabulary includes: polynomial, degree, leading coefficient, end behavior, zeros, multiplicity, turning point. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify answers. Common errors include: computational mistakes, misapplication of formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning.

skills.skill: F: Graph logarithmic functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers graphing logarithmic functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(F)). For f(x) = a*log_b(x-h) + k: identify vertical asymptote x = h, find key points by substituting convenient x-values, note y-intercept if it exists. The parent log function passes through (1, 0) and (b, 1). Transformations shift, stretch, and reflect. Vocabulary: logarithmic function, vertical asymptote, base, transformation, inverse. Bases 2, 10, and e (ln) are most common. Common errors: graphing on wrong side of asymptote, forgetting the vertical asymptote, and incorrect key points.

skills.skill: F: Graph sine functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers graph sine functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(F)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: F: Graph cosine functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers graph cosine functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(F)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: F: Graph sine and cosine functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers graph sine and cosine functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(F)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: G: graph functions, including exponential, logarithmic, sine, cosine, rational, polynomial, and power functions and their transformations, including af(x), f(x) + d, f(x - c), f(bx) for specific values of a, b, c, and d, in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers graph functions, including exponential, logarithmic, sine, cosine, rational, polynomial, and power functions and their transformations, including af(x), f(x) + d, f(x (c), f(bx) for specific values of a, b, c, and d, in mathematical and real-world problems) for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(G)). Function transformations: af(x) vertical stretch/compression, f(x)+d vertical shift, f(x-c) horizontal shift, f(bx) horizontal stretch/compression. Key vocabulary includes: compression, dilation, reflection, stretch, transformation, translation. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: G: Function transformation rules
skills.prompt: This topic covers function transformation rules for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(G)). Function transformations: af(x) vertical stretch/compression, f(x)+d vertical shift, f(x-c) horizontal shift, f(bx) horizontal stretch/compression. Key vocabulary includes: compression, dilation, reflection, stretch, transformation, translation. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: G: Translations of functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers translations of functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(G)). f(x)+d shifts up (d>0) or down (d<0). f(x-c) shifts right (c>0) or left (c<0). Translations preserve shape but change position. Equations, graphs, and tables all shift accordingly. Key vocabulary: translation, horizontal shift, vertical shift, function. Common errors: confusing direction of horizontal shifts (f(x-c) shifts right), and not shifting all features.

skills.skill: G: Reflections of functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers reflections of functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(G)). -f(x) reflects over x-axis (negate outputs). f(-x) reflects over y-axis (negate inputs). Combining: -f(-x) reflects over both axes (equivalent to 180-degree rotation). Key vocabulary: reflection, x-axis, y-axis, negate, symmetry. Common errors: confusing which axis the reflection is over, and not applying to all points.

skills.skill: G: Dilations of functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers dilations of functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(G)). af(x): |a|>1 vertical stretch, 0<|a|<1 vertical compression. f(bx): |b|>1 horizontal compression, 0<|b|<1 horizontal stretch (counterintuitive). Negative a or b adds reflection. Key vocabulary: dilation, stretch, compression, vertical, horizontal, scale factor. Common errors: confusing stretch/compression for horizontal dilations, and not handling negative parameters.

skills.skill: G: Transformations of functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers transformations of functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(G)). Function transformations: af(x) vertical stretch/compression, f(x)+d vertical shift, f(x-c) horizontal shift, f(bx) horizontal stretch/compression. Key vocabulary includes: compression, dilation, reflection, stretch, transformation, translation. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.prompt: This topic covers describe function transformations for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(G)). Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. skills.skill: H: graph arcsin x and arccos x and describe the limitations on the domain
skills.prompt: This topic covers graph arcsin x and arccos x and describe the limitations on the domain for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(H)). arcsin(x) has domain [-1,1] and range [-pi/2, pi/2]. arccos(x) has domain [-1,1] and range [0, pi]. These are inverse trig functions with restricted domains. Key vocabulary includes: arcsin, arccos, inverse trigonometric, domain, range, restriction. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify answers. Common errors include: computational mistakes, misapplication of formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning.

skills.skill: I: determine and analyze the key features of exponential, logarithmic, rational, polynomial, power, trigonometric, inverse trigonometric, and piecewise defined functions, including step functions such as domain, range, symmetry, relative maximum, relative minimum, zeros, asymptotes, and intervals over which the function is increasing or decreasing
skills.prompt: This topic covers evaluating piecewise-defined functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). A piecewise function has different rules for different domain intervals. To evaluate: determine which piece applies based on the input value, then use that formula. Check boundary points carefully for which piece includes them. Example: f(x) = {x+1 if x<0; 2x if x>=0}. Vocabulary: piecewise function, domain restriction, boundary, interval. Common errors: using wrong piece, misreading inequalities at boundaries, and not evaluating boundary points correctly.

skills.skill: I: Find the roots of factored polynomials
skills.prompt: This topic covers factoring polynomials for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). Techniques include: GCF extraction, grouping, Rational Root Theorem with synthetic division, sum/difference of cubes, quadratic patterns, and trial factors. Strategy: always extract GCF first, then choose technique based on structure. A polynomial of degree n has at most n linear factors. Vocabulary: factor, GCF, grouping, rational root, synthetic division, irreducible. Common errors: not extracting GCF first, missing rational roots, and incomplete factorization.

skills.skill: I: Match polynomials and graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers match polynomials and graphs for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). Match/graph polynomials by analyzing: degree (determines end behavior and max turning points), leading coefficient (determines direction), zeros/x-intercepts, y-intercept, and multiplicity of roots. Key vocabulary includes: polynomial, degree, leading coefficient, end behavior, zeros, multiplicity, turning point. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify answers. Common errors include: computational mistakes, misapplication of formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning.

skills.skill: I: Domain and range of polynomials
skills.prompt: This topic covers domain and range of polynomial functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). Domain is always all real numbers. Range: odd-degree polynomials have range all reals; even-degree polynomials have restricted range determined by absolute extrema. Students analyze end behavior and local extrema. Key vocabulary: polynomial, degree, domain, range, end behavior, local extrema. Common errors: assuming all polynomials have range of all reals.

skills.skill: I: Rational functions: asymptotes and excluded values
skills.prompt: This topic covers rational functions (asymptotes and excluded values) for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). Vertical asymptotes occur where the function is undefined and values approach infinity. Horizontal asymptotes describe end behavior. Oblique asymptotes occur when numerator degree exceeds denominator by 1. Key vocabulary includes: behavior, horizontal asymptote, limit, oblique asymptote, vertical asymptote. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: I: Linear and exponential functions over unit intervals
skills.prompt: This topic covers comparing linear and exponential functions over unit intervals for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). Linear functions have constant additive change per unit interval. Exponential functions have constant multiplicative change (constant ratio). Students distinguish by examining differences vs ratios in tables. Key vocabulary: unit interval, constant difference, constant ratio, linear, exponential. Common errors: confusing additive with multiplicative change.

skills.skill: I: Domain and range of logarithmic functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers domain and range of logarithmic functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). For f(x) = a*log_b(x-h) + k: domain is (h, infinity) because the argument must be positive, range is all real numbers. The vertical asymptote is x = h. The logarithmic function is the inverse of exponential, so domain/range are swapped. Vocabulary: logarithmic function, domain, range, vertical asymptote, argument. Common errors: including the asymptote in the domain, incorrectly stating range is restricted, and sign errors finding h.

skills.skill: I: Find properties of sine functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers find properties of sine functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: I: Find properties of cosine functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers find properties of cosine functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: I: Symmetry and periodicity of trigonometric functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers symmetry and periodicity of trigonometric functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(I)). Sine is odd: sin(-x)=-sin(x). Cosine is even: cos(-x)=cos(x). Sin and cos have period 2pi; tan has period pi. Students use these properties to simplify expressions and evaluate functions. Key vocabulary: symmetry, periodicity, even, odd, period, 2pi, pi. Common errors: confusing even/odd properties, and not recognizing periodicity.

skills.skill: J: analyze and describe end behavior of functions, including exponential, logarithmic, rational, polynomial, and power functions, using infinity notation to communicate this characteristic in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers analyze and describe end behavior of functions, including exponential, logarithmic, rational, polynomial, and power functions, using infinity notation to communicate this characteristic in mathematical and real-world problems for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(J)). End behavior describes function values as x approaches +/- infinity, determined by the leading term for polynomials. Use infinity notation: as x -> infinity, f(x) -> ... Key vocabulary includes: degree, end behavior, infinity, leading coefficient, leading term. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: K: analyze characteristics of rational functions and the behavior of the function around the asymptotes, including horizontal, vertical, and oblique asymptotes
skills.prompt: This topic covers analyze characteristics of rational functions and the behavior of the function around the asymptotes, including horizontal, vertical, and oblique asymptotes for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(K)). Vertical asymptotes occur where the function is undefined and values approach infinity. Horizontal asymptotes describe end behavior. Oblique asymptotes occur when numerator degree exceeds denominator by 1. Key vocabulary includes: behavior, horizontal asymptote, limit, oblique asymptote, vertical asymptote. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: K: Rational functions: asymptotes and excluded values
skills.prompt: This topic covers rational functions (asymptotes and excluded values) for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(K)). Oblique asymptotes occur when numerator degree exceeds denominator by 1. Key vocabulary includes: behavior, horizontal asymptote, limit, oblique asymptote, vertical asymptote. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. skills.skill: K: Find the limit at a vertical asymptote of a rational function I
skills.prompt: This topic covers find the limit at a vertical asymptote of a rational function i for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(K)). Vertical asymptotes occur where the function is undefined and values approach infinity. Horizontal asymptotes describe end behavior. Oblique asymptotes occur when numerator degree exceeds denominator by 1. Key vocabulary includes: behavior, horizontal asymptote, limit, oblique asymptote, vertical asymptote. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: K: Find the limit at a vertical asymptote of a rational function II
skills.prompt: This topic covers find the limit at a vertical asymptote of a rational function ii for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(K)). Vertical asymptotes occur where the function is undefined and values approach infinity. Horizontal asymptotes describe end behavior. Oblique asymptotes occur when numerator degree exceeds denominator by 1. Key vocabulary includes: behavior, horizontal asymptote, limit, oblique asymptote, vertical asymptote. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: L: determine various types of discontinuities in the interval (-∞, ∞) as they relate to functions and explore the limitations of the graphing calculator as it relates to the behavior of the function around discontinuities
skills.prompt: This topic covers determine various types of discontinuities in the interval (-∞, ∞) as they relate to functions and explore the limitations of the graphing calculator as it relates to the behavior of the function around discontinuities for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(L)). Types of discontinuity: removable (hole), jump (left and right limits differ), and infinite (vertical asymptote). Students analyze behavior near discontinuities. Key vocabulary includes: asymptote, discontinuity, hole, infinite, jump, removable. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: L: Rational functions: asymptotes and excluded values
skills.prompt: This topic covers rational functions (asymptotes and excluded values) for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(L)). Vertical asymptotes occur where the function is undefined and values approach infinity. Horizontal asymptotes describe end behavior. Oblique asymptotes occur when numerator degree exceeds denominator by 1. Key vocabulary includes: behavior, horizontal asymptote, limit, oblique asymptote, vertical asymptote. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: L: Find and analyze points of discontinuity using graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers find and analyze points of discontinuity using graphs for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(L)). Types of discontinuity: removable (hole), jump (left and right limits differ), and infinite (vertical asymptote). Students analyze behavior near discontinuities. Key vocabulary includes: asymptote, discontinuity, hole, infinite, jump, removable. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: M: describe the left-sided behavior and the right-sided behavior of the graph of a function around discontinuities
skills.prompt: This topic covers describe the left-sided behavior and the right-sided behavior of the graph of a function around discontinuities for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(M)). Types of discontinuity: removable (hole), jump (left and right limits differ), and infinite (vertical asymptote). Students analyze behavior near discontinuities. Key vocabulary includes: asymptote, discontinuity, hole, infinite, jump, removable. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: N: analyze situations modeled by functions, including exponential, logarithmic, rational, polynomial, and power functions, to solve real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers analyzing real-world situations modeled by various functions for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(N)). Students select appropriate function type (exponential, logarithmic, rational, polynomial, power) to model data, solve problems, and interpret results. Key vocabulary: model, function, real-world, exponential, logarithmic, polynomial, interpret. Common errors: choosing wrong function type, and not interpreting results in context.

skills.skill: N: Exponential growth and decay: word problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers exponential growth and decay word problems for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(N)). Students apply f(t) = a(1+r)^t for growth and f(t) = a(1-r)^t for decay, where a is initial value, r is rate, t is time. Growth factor b = 1+r (e.g., 5% growth gives b = 1.05). Decay factor b = 1-r (e.g., 3% decay gives b = 0.97). Key concepts: doubling time, half-life, percentage change. Vocabulary: exponential growth, exponential decay, growth factor, decay factor, half-life. Common errors: confusing rate with factor, using rate as b instead of 1+/-rate, and not converting percentages to decimals.

skills.skill: N: Compound interest: word problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers compound interest word problems for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(N)). Formula: A=P(1+r/n)^(nt) for discrete, A=Pe^(rt) for continuous. Solve for any variable; solving for t requires logarithms. Key vocabulary: principal, compound interest, continuous, e, logarithm. Common errors: confusing formulas, not converting rates, and not using logarithms for t.

skills.skill: N: Exponential regression
skills.prompt: This topic covers exponential regression for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(N)). Students use technology to find y = ab^x that best fits data. Steps: enter data, run exponential regression, interpret a and b, use for predictions. Correlation coefficient r indicates fit quality. Students compare exponential vs linear models. Vocabulary: regression, exponential model, correlation coefficient, residual, best fit, interpolation, extrapolation. Common errors: using model far outside data range, confusing with linear regression, and misinterpreting R^2.

skills.skill: O: develop and use a sinusoidal function that models a situation in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers develop and use a sinusoidal function that models a situation in mathematical and real-world problems for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(O)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: O: Write equations of sine functions from graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers write equations of sine functions from graphs for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(O)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.prompt: This topic covers write equations of cosine functions from graphs for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(O)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: O: Write equations of cosine functions using properties
skills.prompt: This topic covers write equations of cosine functions using properties for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(O)). Sinusoidal functions: y = a*sin(b(x-c)) + d or y = a*cos(b(x-c)) + d. Amplitude = |a|, period = 2*pi/|b|, phase shift = c, vertical shift = d. Key vocabulary includes: amplitude, period, phase shift, sinusoidal, vertical shift. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. skills.skill: P: determine the values of the trigonometric functions at the special angles and relate them in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers determine the values of the trigonometric functions at the special angles and relate them in mathematical and real-world problems for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(P)). Evaluate trig functions at special angles (0, 30, 45, 60, 90 degrees or 0, pi/6, pi/4, pi/3, pi/2 radians) using special right triangles or the unit circle. Key vocabulary includes: special angles, unit circle, exact values, sine, cosine, tangent. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify answers. Common errors include: computational mistakes, misapplication of formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning.

skills.skill: P: Find trigonometric ratios using right triangles
skills.prompt: This topic covers find trigonometric ratios using right triangles for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(P)). In right triangles: sin = opposite/hypotenuse, cos = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan = opposite/adjacent (SOHCAHTOA). Find sides or angles. In right triangles: sin = opp/hyp, cos = adj/hyp, tan = opp/adj. Given two sides or one side and an angle, find remaining parts. Key vocabulary includes: right triangle, sine, cosine, tangent, SOHCAHTOA. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify answers. Common errors include: computational mistakes, misapplication of formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning.

skills.skill: P: Find trigonometric ratios using reference angles
skills.prompt: This topic covers find trigonometric ratios using reference angles for Precalculus students (TEKS P.2(P)). Coterminal angles differ by multiples of 360 degrees (or 2*pi). Reference angle is the acute angle to the x-axis, used to evaluate trig functions in any quadrant. Key vocabulary includes: coterminal, quadrant, reference angle, standard position, terminal side. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Precalculus. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.
